definition: The Spiritual Flood — the outpouring of divine grace that Shaykh Ibrāhīm Niasse identified as the defining spiritual event of the age, predicted in the traditions of Shaykh Aḥmad al-Tijānī. Mishri bears witness to Niasse as 'the owner of the Fayḍa that Shaykh Tijānī had mentioned' — his personal recognition of Niasse's claim constitutes one of the primary testimonies of authorisation within the tradition. In Niasse's tafsīr, the term fayḍa appears in its grammatical root (fayḍa — outpouring) in Lesson 27: 'when another outpouring (fayḍa) of divine mercy floods over it [the soul], it becomes inspired' (Q. 12:53–13:18). This usage situates the Fayḍa within Niasse's broader theology of divine mercy overflowing into the receptive soul — the Fayḍa movement is the collective realisation of what happens to the individual soul when grace descends.
mishri_quote: ...the owner of the Faydah that Shaykh Tijani had mentioned, in addition to other matters that neither pen nor paper could do justice.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: فإذا فاضت عليها فيضة أخرى من رحمة اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى تكون ملهمة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: When another outpouring (fayḍa) of divine mercy floods over it [the soul], it becomes inspired

definition: Spiritual education and formation — the initiatic method by which a murshid guides a disciple toward the realisation of tawḥīd and maʿrifa. Mishri testifies personally to receiving tarbiya at Niasse's hands: 'I went to Shaykh Ibrahim, who renewed the wird for me, and I received tarbiya at his hands.' This testimony establishes tarbiya as the foundational act of the Tijānī-Niassene path — the transmission of spiritual capacity through direct contact with the Shaykh. In Niasse's own tafsīr, tarbiya is described as the function that every divine sign and saintly miracle serves: 'every miracle of a prophet and every karāma of a wali, whether of the sciences or otherwise, is a tarbiya for the people of his time — whoever is prepared receives it at the first indication and benefits' (Lesson 17, Q. 6:111–165). Tarbiya is thus simultaneously personal (the Shaykh's formation of the individual disciple) and cosmological (God's formation of the community through His signs).
mishri_quote: I woke up and went to Shaykh Ibrahim, who renewed the wird for me, and I received tarbiyah at his hands.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: كل معجزة لنبي وكل كرامة لولي سواء علمية كانت أو غيرها فهي تربية لأهل زمانه، فمن كان مستعدا يترى بأول إشارة وينتفع
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Every miracle of a prophet and every karāma of a wali is a tarbiya for the people of his time — whoever is prepared receives it at the first indication and benefits

definition: Divine unity — the foundational Islamic doctrine, which Niasse's tafsīr practises in its fullest sense across all thirty lessons. Al-Tijānī distinguishes two modes of tawḥīd that illuminate Niasse's exegetical method: the prophetic tawḥīd (tawḥīd al-rusul), which is lived and experiential — the tawḥīd with which all messengers were sent and which the Companions preserved intact — and the kalām tawḥīd of the theologians, which al-Tijānī identifies as a philosophical deviation that took hold in the third generation. Niasse's tafsīr enacts the former: it does not argue for God's unity rationally but demonstrates it through simultaneous engagement with legal, spiritual, narrative, and linguistic dimensions of the Qurʾānic text. In Niasse's own words, the Qurʾān presents tawḥīd, rulings, and narratives together because 'patience with one kind of thing is difficult for the children of Adam' (Lesson 7). Its most precise theological formulation occurs in Lesson 12: 'the servant becomes annihilated, existence becomes annihilated, and the real tawḥīd (al-tawḥīd al-ḥaqīqī) and eternal life are attained' — placing tawḥīd at the apex of the Sufi path as the experiential realisation of divine unity, not its rational demonstration.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: فيفنى العبد ويفنى الوجود ويحصل التوحيد الحقيقي والحياة الأبدية
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The servant becomes annihilated, existence becomes annihilated, and the real tawḥīd (al-tawḥīd al-ḥaqīqī) and eternal life are attained

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: ذكر القصص يذكر الأحكام ويذكر التوحيد لأن الصبر على نوع واحد صعب على بني آدم
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The Qurʾān mentions narratives, then rulings, then tawḥīd — because patience with one kind of thing alone is difficult for the children of Adam

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: والتذكير به على لسان صاحب المعجزة قائم مقام ذكره في النفوس، تذكرة التوحيد
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Reminding of God through the tongue of the one who bears miracles stands in place of that remembrance in souls — a reminder of tawḥīd

mishri_quote: The path to [prophetic tawḥīd] was abandoned and eventually effaced only because the general public turned away from it, inclining instead toward the rhetorical embellishments of kalām theology... Those who continued to walk the path of the messengers' tawḥīd came to be known as Ṣūfīs and ʿārifīn.
mishri_source: Shaykh Aḥmad al-Tijānī (d. 1815), as reported in Muḥammad al-Mishri, Kitāb al-Jāmiʿ, pp. 66–67

definition: Annihilation — the dissolution of the ego-self in the consciousness of divine reality. Mishri defines it in relation to baqāʾ (subsistence) and locates it within the framework of the ḥaḍarāt: genuine Iman at its lowest level entails 'fanāʾ of the nafs and baqāʾ in Allah in the ḥaḍarāt of His Names and His Attributes.' In Niasse's own commentary, fanāʾ proceeds in three degrees: annihilation in the divine acts (fanāʾ fī al-afʿāl), then in the divine attributes (fanāʾ fī al-ṣifāt), then total annihilation (al-fanāʾ al-kullī) — after which 'his annihilation was identical to subsistence' (Lesson 10, compiler's apparatus, fn. 3).

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: فمن بذل ماله لله وجد الفناء في الأفعال، يعلم أن لا فاعل في الوجود إلا الله
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Whoever gives his wealth for God finds annihilation in the acts — knowing there is no agent in existence but God

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: بذكر القلب يجد الفناء في الصفات، وبذكر الروح يجد الفناء الكلي
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on three degrees of fanāʾ: in acts (afʿāl), attributes (ṣifāt), and totality (kullī)

mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)
mishri_quote: The lowest degree of [true Iman] is fanāʾ of the nafs and baqāʾ in Allah in the ḥaḍarāt of His Names and His Attributes.

definition: Subsistence — the station that follows and completes fanāʾ (annihilation). The self does not cease to exist but subsists in and through the divine reality. As the compiler's apparatus records of Niasse's teaching: 'his annihilation was identical to subsistence' (fanāʾuhu ʿayn al-baqāʾ). The pair fanāʾ/baqāʾ constitutes the fundamental dyad of Sufi anthropology in Niasse's system, and Mishri places both within the ḥaḍarāt of the divine Names and Attributes — locating the spiritual ascent within a precise cosmological framework.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)
mishri_quote: The lowest degree of true Iman is fanāʾ of the nafs and baqāʾ in Allah in the ḥaḍarāt of His Names and His Attributes.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: ألْحَىُّ الدائم البقاء ألْفَيُّومٌ المبالغ في القيومية
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Al-Ḥayy — the Eternally Subsistent (al-Baqāʾ); al-Qayyūm — the Self-Sustaining in the extreme

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: لم يعط هذا ولكن أعطي البقاء إلى آخر الدهر
niasse_evidence.translation_note: He was not given that, but was given subsistence (baqāʾ) unto the end of time

definition: Sainthood — the station of proximity to God and spiritual authority that follows from the full realisation of tawḥīd. Mishri specifies the inaugural act of wilāya: 'the first step a wali takes in wilāya is the killing of the nafs' — meaning that wilāya is not a passive state but an active, ongoing combat against the ego-self. In Niasse's tafsīr, wilāya is graduated: he distinguishes between wilāya ṣughrā (the minor wilāya — anyone characterised by a single Qurʾānically praised quality) and wilāya kubrā (the major wilāya — maʿrifa of God exceeding faith and certainty, the wilāya of direct vision) (Lesson 24, apparatus). His most explicit use in the body text appears in Lesson 13, where he describes ʿĪsā (Jesus) as 'sealing the Muhammadan wilāya (khātim al-wilāya al-muḥammadiyya) as an honour to this community, so that no wilāya remains after the descent of Jesus' — positioning the sealing of wilāya within the eschatological framework of the tafsīr.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: خاتما للولاية المحمدية تشريفا لهذه الأمة، فلا ولاية بعد نزول عيسى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Sealing the Muhammadan wilāya as an honour to this community — no wilāya remains after the descent of Jesus

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: ن اتصف بصفة من الصفات التي مدحها القرآن فهو من الأولياء هذه الولاية الصغرى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Whoever is characterised by a quality the Qurʾān praises — he is among the awliyāʾ. This is the minor wilāya (wilāya ṣughrā)

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: أولياء اللّٰه بالولاية الصغرى... وأولياء اللّٰه بالولاية الكبرى
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler distinguishing wilāya ṣughrā (minor sainthood) from wilāya kubrā (major sainthood)

mishri_quote: The first step a wali takes in walaya is the killing of the nafs. Then he struggles against other nufus... The people of nufus are regimented soldiers who know their enemies and are constantly striving against anyone who threatens their dominance.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

definition: Spiritual station — a stable, attained level of realisation on the path toward God, as distinguished from ḥāl (a transient spiritual state). Mishri illuminates the epistemological significance of the maqām: a wali who speaks from the maqām of slavehood (ʿubūda) uses attributes of deficiency to describe himself, which the uninitiated misread as false piety — 'the denier has no choice but to say, he just said what he did to show everyone how pious he is.' This makes the maqām the hermeneutical key to the wali's speech: the maqām from which a statement is made determines its meaning. In Niasse's tafsīr, the maqāmāt are both spiritual and exegetical: 'for every maqām there is a corresponding utterance' (Lesson 28). His most explicit treatment appears in Lesson 9, where he enumerates the maqāmāt in relation to the spiritual path. The highest is named in Lesson 28: 'the maqām of recollection (tatadhakkur)', which succeeds reflection (tafakkur) and constitutes the apex of spiritual cognition.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: من حاول الوصول إلى الله تبارك وتعالى وادعى المقامات بغير محبة أولياء الله
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Whoever attempts to reach God and claims the maqāmāt without love of God's awliyāʾ

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: الروح هو السلطان والعقل هو الوزير والقلب هو المفتي. إذا اتفق هؤلاء
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The rūḥ is the sovereign, the intellect is the vizier, the qalb is the jurist — when these three agree

mishri_quote: If the wali speaks from the maqam of selfless slavehood and uses attributes of deficiency to describe him- or herself, the denier has no choice but to say, 'He just said what he did to show everyone how pious he is.' That is how the denier thinks.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

definition: Inimitability — the doctrine that the Quran cannot be matched or replicated by human composition, establishing its miraculous nature. Niasse engages this doctrine at length in his discussion of the opening suras, situating it within the broader tradition of Quranic sciences.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: كل هذا إعجاز. ولكن تحداهم بالإعجاز حيث أنهم لم يأتوا بعشر سور مثله في الفصاحة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: All of this is inimitability (iʿjāz). He challenged them with it — they could not produce ten suras like it in eloquence

definition: The disjointed letters — the letter-combinations that open twenty-nine Quranic suras (Alif Lām Mīm, Yā Sīn, etc.). Niasse discusses multiple interpretive traditions — grammatical, mystical, and prophetic — and gives his own reading, which tends toward the Sufi understanding of these letters as keys to divine secrets.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: أسرار ألقاها اللّٰه في كتابه لا يعلم أحد معناها إلا من استحق علمه، يلهمه اللّٰه ولا يكتب في الأوراق ولا ينظر بالأحداق
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Secrets that God has placed in His Book — no one knows their meaning except one who has earned that knowledge; God inspires it in him, and it is not written on pages nor seen with eyes

definition: Direct, experiential knowledge of God — the supreme object of the spiritual path in Niasse's Tijānī tradition. Mishri distinguishes maʿrifa from mere doctrinal knowledge: the Qurʾān possesses an outward dimension (the foundation of the sharīʿa, governing right action) and an inward dimension (the foundation of the ḥaqīqa, which is the domain of maʿrifa). While the sharīʿa is sealed and admits no addition, the inward undergoes 'constant renewal in regards to how its meanings become manifest' for the ʿārifūn — those who have attained maʿrifa. In Niasse's own usage, maʿrifa is 'the spirit of the intellect, and the intellect is the spirit of the body' (Lesson 16, Q. 6:36–110), situating it at the apex of the spiritual faculties hierarchy.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: والجنة المعنوية هي المعرفة بالله تبارك وتعالى ومن دخلها لا يشتاق إلى الجنة المستقبلة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The inward Garden is maʿrifa of God — whoever enters it does not yearn for the future Garden

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: المعرفة روح روح الروح، المعرفة روح العقل، والعقل روح الجسد
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Maʿrifa is the spirit of the spirit of the spirit; maʿrifa is the spirit of the intellect, and the intellect is the spirit of the body

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: ولاية الكبرى من عرف اللّٰه معرفة فوق الإيمان وفوق الإيقان بل معرفة المشاهدة والعيان
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler citing Niasse's distinction between levels of maʿrifa

mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018), Q. 17 (pp. 36–37).
mishri_quote: Allah's speech... has an outward and an inward: its outward is the foundation of the sharīʿa, and its inward is the foundation of the haqīqah... constant renewal happens in regards to how its meanings become manifest.

definition: The innermost secret — the highest of the spiritual faculties, corresponding in Mishri's framework to the divine Name al-Awwal (the First). Mishri articulates the full correspondence: 'if the quṭb were to say, I have a firstness, which is my sirr; a lastness, which is my rūḥ; a manifestness, which is my jism; and a hiddenness, which is my qalb — we would say to him, nothing of existence remains yours.' This is the complete absorption of the self into the divine Names: the sirr as al-Awwal, the rūḥ as al-Ākhir, the jism as al-Ẓāhir, the qalb as al-Bāṭin. The conclusion — nothing of existence remains yours — names the state of total annihilation (fanāʾ) that the sirr makes possible. In Niasse's own tafsīr, the sirr occupies the apex of the spiritual faculty hierarchy: 'the drinking-place of the sirr is intimacy (uns) and awe (hayba)' (Lesson 4, Vol. 1, p. 72), and 'the sirr becomes annihilated in the divine Essence (dhāt Allāh)' (Lesson 6, Vol. 1, p. 120). In Lesson 21, the full ascending sequence is given: 'dhikr must proceed — tongue, then qalb, then rūḥ, then sirr — until it reaches the sought.'

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: مشرب السر الأنس والهيبة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The drinking-place of the sirr is intimacy (uns) and awe (hayba)

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: والسر يفنى في ذات اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The sirr becomes annihilated in the divine Essence

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: ولابد من الذكر باللسان أولا ثم بالقلب ثم بالروح والسر حتى يصل إلى المطلوب
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Dhikr must proceed — tongue, then qalb, then rūḥ, then sirr — until it reaches the sought

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: لباس النفس ولباس القلب ولباس الروح ولباس السر
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on the four vestments of spiritual faculties: nafs, qalb, rūḥ, sirr

mishri_quote: If the qutb were to say to us, 'I have a firstness, which is my sirr; a lastness, which is my ruh; a manifestness, which is my jism; and a hiddenness, which is my heart,' we would say to him, 'Nothing of existence remains yours.'
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

definition: The spirit — the higher faculty that, in contest with the nafs (ego-self), determines the spiritual condition of the human being. Mishri identifies the rūḥ and the nafs as permanent companions in every person: 'until either the nafs dies or the ruh dies, they will remain companions and no human will be free of their contesting with each other.' For the rūḥ to be alive and the nafs to be dead is, in Mishri's formulation, 'real life after which there is no death.' Niasse maps the spiritual faculties in a hierarchy: 'the drinking-place of the spirit (rūḥ) is the vision of Beauty (jamāl) and Majesty (jalāl)' (Lesson 4), and positions the rūḥ above the qalb: 'the rūḥ is the sovereign, the intellect is the vizier, and the qalb is the jurist' (Lesson 27). His most compressed formulation places maʿrifa at the apex of this structure: 'maʿrifa is the spirit of the intellect, and the intellect is the spirit of the body' (Lesson 16).

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: الروح هو السلطان والعقل هو الوزير والقلب هو المفتي
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The rūḥ is the sovereign, the intellect is the vizier, and the qalb is the jurist

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: المعرفة روح العقل، والعقل روح الجسد
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Maʿrifa is the spirit of the intellect, and the intellect is the spirit of the body

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: مشرب الروح مشاهدة الجمال والجلال
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The drinking-place of the rūḥ is the vision of Beauty and Majesty

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: ولباس الروح التقوى ولكن تقوى يمنعه من الالتفات إلى غير اللّٰه
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on the vestment of the rūḥ: taqwā that prevents turning toward other than God

mishri_quote: Until either the nafs dies or the ruh dies, they will remain companions and no human will be free of their contesting with each other. For the ruh to be alive and the nafs to be dead — that is the real life after which there is no death.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

definition: The Muhammadan Reality — the first of the divine self-disclosures (tajalliyyāt) and the metaphysical principle through which creation proceeds. Mishri provides a full cosmological account: 'To bring these ḥaqāʾiq and nisbahs into existence, Allah brought the Ḥaqīqa Muḥammadiyya into existence, for were it not for that, Allah's relation to creation would not have appeared or been known.' It is the 'shadow' of the Aḥadiyya, the locus of the divine Names and their sealhood (khatmiyya), and the barzakh between the pure divine and the created order. In Niasse's tafsīr, it is the first tajallī: 'When God willed to manifest an existence other than His own, He disclosed Himself in the Ḥaqīqa Muḥammadiyya — it is the first tajallī after sheer hiddenness' (Lesson 13, Q. 4:148–5:22).

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: لما أراد إظهار وجود غير وجوده تجلى في الحقيقة المحمدية فهي أول تجلٍ بعد الغيب المحض
niasse_evidence.translation_note: When God willed to manifest an existence other than His own, He disclosed Himself in the Muhammadan Reality — it is the first tajallī after sheer hiddenness

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: هذا الروح هو الحقيقة المحمدية
niasse_evidence.translation_note: This spirit is the Muhammadan Reality

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى أول ما أوجد درة بيضاء وهي الحقيقة المحمدية
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The first thing God brought into existence was a white pearl — that is the Muhammadan Reality

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: هذا الروح هو الذي به ضل النصارى
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler contextualising the Muhammadan Reality against Christian deviation

mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018), Q. 15.
mishri_quote: To bring these ḥaqāʾiq into existence, Allah brought the Ḥaqīqa Muḥammadiyya into existence, for were it not for that, Allah's relation to creation would not have appeared or been known.

definition: The Divine Presence — in Mishri's account, the term denotes the metaphysical stations or modes through which the divine self-disclosure (tajallī) operates. The ḥaḍarāt are the loci through which the Qurʾān 'descended' and through which the divine Names and Attributes are manifest. Mishri connects them directly to the four ḥaqāʾiq: the ḥaqīqa Muḥammadiyya, the realm of the Names, the realm of the spirits, and the realm of sensible existence. In Niasse's usage, the ḥaḍara is the spiritual goal of the murīd: 'until you find intimacy and awe in the pavilions of the Divine Presence' (Lesson 23, Q. 9:100–129).

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: قصدوا الحضرة، إذا صدقوا في أنّهم لا يقصدون غير الحضرة فهم لا مقصود عندهم إلا الله
niasse_evidence.translation_note: They aimed for the Divine Presence (ḥaḍra); if they were sincere in seeking nothing but the ḥaḍra, then they have no goal but God

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: إلى أن تجد الأنس والهيبة بسرادقات الحضرة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Until you find intimacy and awe in the pavilions of the Divine Presence

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: إذا ضربنا هذه الأسماء في الحضرات الثلاث الحقائق الثلاث
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler on the three ḥaḍarāt — the three divine presences

mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018), Q. 15.
mishri_quote: The Exalted Essence was and there was nothing other than it... It wanted to be known, so it manifested in itself, for itself, through itself.

definition: Spiritual wayfaring — the active traversal of the maqāmāt (spiritual stations) through the ḥaḍarāt (divine presences) that constitutes the Sufi path of realisation. Mishri describes sulūk as the mode of engagement that follows the initial state of attraction (jadhb): 'when jadhb is complete and they travel the path of sulūk, they see the verse (āya) in front of them indicating sulūk, the multiplicity of ḥaqāʾiq, the distinction of nisbahs.' Critically, Mishri links sulūk to an expanding understanding of the Qurʾān: 'for each maqām the sālik reaches in any ḥaḍra, they find the verse ahead of them translating for them what they are currently experiencing — neither the understanding of God nor the understanding of the verse has an end.' Niasse states the minimum requirement explicitly: 'Whoever claims to have reached God without sulūk — those are the wrongdoers (ẓālimūn)' (Lesson 9); and distinguishes the sulūk of prophets from that of others: 'the sulūk of non-prophets proceeds from the commanding soul (nafs ammāra) upward, while the sulūk of prophets begins from the tranquil soul (nafs muṭmaʾinna)' (Lesson 27).

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: بدعوى الوصول إلى اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى بدون السلوك فَأَوْلَيِكَ هُمْ ألظَلِمُونَ
niasse_evidence.translation_note: Claiming to reach God without sulūk — those are the wrongdoers

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: سلوك غير الأنبياء من النفس الأمارة بالسوء إلى أن تصل إلى الكمال، وسلوك الأنبياء من النفس المطمئنة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The sulūk of non-prophets proceeds from the commanding soul; the sulūk of prophets begins from the tranquil soul

apparatus_evidence.arabic_quote: الإخوان في السلوك المريدون الصادقون ينتفعون بعضهم من بعض
apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler: brothers in sulūk — sincere murīdūn benefit one another

mishri_quote: When jadhb is complete and they travel the path of suluk, they see the ayah in front of them indicating suluk, the multiplicity of haqa'iq, the distinction of nisbahs, and dispersion. For each new maqam the saliks reach in any hadrah, they find the ayah ahead of them translating for them what they are currently experiencing — neither the understanding of God nor the understanding of the verse has an end.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

definition: Outpouring — the metaphysical and historical effusion of divine mercy through which God's support reaches creation. Al-Tijānī uses the term in two registers that are present simultaneously in Niasse's tafsīr. In the cosmological register, al-Tijānī states: 'God decreed, in His pre-eternal will, that the support that reaches His creation from the outpouring (fayḍ) of His mercy — in every era — should circulate among the elect exalted people among His creation, from among the Prophets and the Ṣiddīqūn.' The fayḍ is thus the channel of divine mercy that flows through the hierarchy of saints and prophets to reach creation in every age. In the historical register, al-Tijānī prophesied the coming of the Fayḍa: 'an effulgence (fayḍa) will come to my Companions, until people will come to our path in waves' — the single occurrence of flooding that would define Niasse's movement. In Niasse's own tafsīr, the term appears in Lesson 27 in its grammatical sense: 'when another outpouring (fayḍa) of divine mercy floods over the soul, it becomes inspired' (Q. 12:53) — using the root فاض to describe the divine mercy overflowing onto the receptive soul, the metaphysical dynamic that underlies both the cosmological and historical senses of al-fayḍa.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: فإذا فاضت عليها فيضة أخرى من رحمة اللّٰه تبارك وتعالى تكون ملهمة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: When another outpouring (fayḍa) of divine mercy floods over the soul, it becomes inspired — the metaphysical dynamic of fayḍ in Niasse's commentary

apparatus_evidence.note: Compiler citing Ibn ʿArabī on fayḍ via Fayḍ al-Qadīr

mishri_quote: God decreed, in His pre-eternal will, that the support that reaches His creation from the outpouring of His mercy — in every era — should circulate among the Elect Exalted people among His creation, from among the Prophets and the Ṣiddīqūn. Whoever takes refuge with the living Elect Exalted people of his era, keeps their company, follows them, and seeks assistance from them, will succeed at attaining the support that outpours from God.
mishri_source: Shaykh Aḥmad al-Tijānī (d. 1815), as reported in Muḥammad al-Mishri, Kitāb al-Jāmiʿ

definition: Vigilant-awareness — the sustained consciousness of being seen by God, which Mishri identifies as the primary differentiator of spiritual rank. Murāqaba is the station below mushāhada (direct vision), and Niasse positions it as the minimum attainment of the Sufi path: 'If you cannot reach the station of mushāhada, at least reach the station of murāqaba' (Lesson 30, Q. 16:90–17:111). Mishri elaborates: 'People differ in their standing with Allah according to the degree of their murāqaba, and the people of hearts are the people of Allah.' The station is practised through attending to God's presence at all times, and is the foundation of all subsequent spiritual realisation.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Words of Mishri (unpublished transcriptions, n.d.), authorised by Shaykh Ibrāhīm Niasse
mishri_quote: People differ in their standing with Allah according to the degree of their murāqabah, and the people of hearts are the people of Allah. Whoever wishes to be foremost and not overtaken must hasten toward murāqabah.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: إن لم تقدر أن تصل إلى مرتبة المشاهدة فلا أقل من أن تصل إلى مرتبة المراقبة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: If you cannot reach the station of mushāhada (direct vision), at least reach the station of murāqaba

definition: Spiritual aspiration — the directed force of the soul toward God and toward God's people. In Niasse's tafsīr sessions, the opening poem of every lesson is an invocation of the Shaykh's himma: 'Yā himmat al-shaykh uḥḍurī / lanā bi-hādhā al-maḥḍar / wa-ltaṭifī bi-naẓra / tātī lanā bi-l-ẓafar' (O aspiration of the Shaykh, be present with us in this gathering; look upon us with a glance that brings us victory). The invocation across all thirty lessons places himma at the threshold of Qurʾānic engagement — it is the spiritual condition of possibility for exegesis. Mishri distinguishes himma from related terms: 'Aspiration (himma) is of three types: zeal (himma), care (ihtimām), and worry (hamma). Whatever is for Allah and in Allah is zeal (himma).' This positions himma as the highest of the three — desire directed entirely toward the divine.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Words of Mishri (unpublished transcriptions, n.d.), authorised by Shaykh Ibrāhīm Niasse
mishri_quote: Aspiration is of three types: zeal (himma), care (ihtimām), and worry (hamma). Whatever is for Allah and in Allah is zeal.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: يا همة الشيخ احضري لنا بهذا المحضر ولتعطفي بنظرة تأتي لنا بالظفر
niasse_evidence.translation_note: O aspiration of the Shaykh, be present with us in this gathering; look upon us with a glance that brings us victory [opening invocation of all 30 lessons]

definition: Repentance and return — the act of turning back to God from heedlessness or transgression. In the Tijānī tradition, tawba is not merely moral amendment but a continuous spiritual orientation: Mishri states that 'any time that passes without murāqabah — let one be certain that what was lost of goodness in that moment exceeds all that was gained throughout one's entire past. Let him return to Allah and reclaim what has passed by saying, Innā lillāh wa-innā ilayhi rājiʿūn.' This frames tawba not as a single act of contrition but as a sustained return. In Niasse's tafsīr, tawba appears across multiple lessons as a condition for spiritual advancement, most explicitly in Lesson 10: 'He had spent his life in error; today I have cut my zunnār and here I am returning to You in tawba.' The formula of return — tawba followed by renewed orientation toward God — is the structuring logic of the Sufi path.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Words of Mishri (unpublished transcriptions, n.d.), authorised by Shaykh Ibrāhīm Niasse
mishri_quote: Any time that passes without murāqabah — let one be certain that what was lost of goodness in that moment exceeds all that was gained throughout one's entire past. Let him return to Allah and reclaim what has passed by saying: Innā lillāh wa-innā ilayhi rājiʿūn.

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: قضى عمره في الضلالة، واليوم قطعت زناري وها أنا رجعت إليك بالتوبة
niasse_evidence.translation_note: He had spent his life in error; today I have cut my zunnār and here I am returning to You in tawba

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: وأراد أن يقبل منا التوبة فنرجع إلى اللٰه تبارك وتعالى
niasse_evidence.translation_note: He willed to accept our tawba so that we return to God

definition: The heart — the spiritual faculty that serves as the locus of discernment, located in Niasse's hierarchy between the rūḥ (spirit) and the nafs (ego-self). Mishri identifies the qalb as the seat of reconciliation between rūḥ and nafs: 'the nafs and the rūḥ only come into agreement in the heart of the perfect wali, to whom they become submissive.' In Niasse's own mapping of the spiritual faculties, the qalb is the jurist (muftī) in the hierarchy where the rūḥ is the sovereign and the intellect is the vizier (Lesson 27). Its drinking-place (mashrab) is fear and hope: 'the drinking-place of the qalb is fear (khawf) and hope (rajāʾ)' (Lesson 4). The qalb is also the site of spiritual light: 'there must be a light (nūr) in the heart of the exegete' (Lesson 1, Vol. 1, p. 29) — positioning it as the prerequisite of Qurʾānic understanding.
mishri_quote: The nafs and the ruh only come into agreement in the heart of the perfect wali, to whom they become submissive; the Iman of the nafs and the ruh will never be complete until the two of them have the wali decide between them whenever they dispute.
mishri_source: Muḥammad al-Mishri, Qanābil Yadawiyya fī al-Dhabb ʿan Ṭarīq al-Ṣūfiyya, trans. Saʿeed Simone Nardella & Adnan Wood-Smith (n.p., 2018)

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: الروح هو السلطان والعقل هو الوزير والقلب هو المفتي
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The rūḥ is the sovereign, the intellect is the vizier, the qalb is the jurist

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: مشرب القلب الخوف والرجاء
niasse_evidence.translation_note: The drinking-place of the qalb is fear (khawf) and hope (rajāʾ)

niasse_evidence.arabic_quote: لابد من نور في قلب المفسر
niasse_evidence.translation_note: There must be a light (nūr) in the heart of the exegete
